title: Solanum dulcamara littorale
taxonomy: Solanum dulcamara littorale is a variant of the climbing nightshade plant, which is known to belong to the family Solanaceae. This family is commonly referred to as the nightshade family and includes other well-known plants such as potatoes and tomatoes. The Solanaceae family is further divided into several subfamilies, including the Solanoideae subfamily. Within the Solanoideae subfamily, Solanum dulcamara littorale is classified as part of the genus Solanum. The classification is based on the plant's physical characteristics, growth habits, and genetic makeup.
uses: I don't have information about the specific medicinal uses of Solanum dulcamara littorale.
benefits: I'm not aware of any health benefits directly associated with Solanum dulcamara littorale.
properties: Solanum dulcamara littorale, also known as seaside bitters or climbing nightshade, has been used in traditional medicine for its therapeutic properties. The plant contains alkaloids, glycosides, and other compounds that may have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. These properties are thought to contribute to the plant's potential uses in treating various health conditions, including wounds, skin irritations, and respiratory issues. However, more research is needed to fully understand the efficacy and safety of using Solanum dulcamara littorale as a treatment for these conditions. In some traditional practices, the plant is also used to treat digestive issues and fever.
constituents: Solanum dulcamara littorale contains a variety of active constituents, including glycosides, alkaloids, and saponins. These compounds may contribute to its potential medicinal properties and effects on the body. The glycosides present in Solanum dulcamara littorale, such as dulcamarine and dulcamarinic acid, have been reported to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities. Alkaloids like solanine and solanidine may also play a role in its pharmacological effects. Further research is necessary to fully understand the active constituents and their impact on human health.
